result:
# The Curious Appeal of Liminal Spaces

There's something haunting about empty airports at 3 AM, or fluorescent-lit hotel hallways that seem to stretch forever. These "liminal spaces" — transitional places between one destination and another — have captured internet fascination in recent years, but I think humans have always felt their strange pull.

Liminal comes from the Latin word for threshold. These are the in-between places: parking garages, stairwells, rest stops on forgotten highways. They're designed to be passed through, not inhabited. Maybe that's why they feel so uncanny when we find ourselves alone in them.

I find myself drawn to the emotional quality of liminality too — not just physical spaces, but temporal ones. That weird week between Christmas and New Year's when time feels suspended. The summer after graduation before real life begins. Sunday evenings. These moments exist in a strange pocket dimension where normal rules don't quite apply.

What fascinates me is why we find these experiences so compelling, even unsettling. Perhaps it's because liminal spaces strip away context and purpose. A mall without shoppers isn't really a mall anymore — it's just architecture, exposed and strange. We see the scaffolding of our world without its usual meaning.

Or maybe liminal spaces resonate because they mirror something true about existence itself. We're all in transit, really, passing through stages and phases, never quite arriving. These empty hallways and quiet hours are just honest about what we usually prefer to ignore: the in-between is where we spend most of our lives.

# The Curious Appeal of Liminal Spaces

There's something haunting about empty airports at 3 AM, or fluorescent-lit hotel hallways that seem to stretch forever. These "liminal spaces" — transitional places between one destination and another — have captured internet fascination in recent years, but I think humans have always felt their strange pull.

Liminal comes from the Latin word for threshold. These are the in-between places: parking garages, stairwells, rest stops on forgotten highways. They're designed to be passed through, not inhabited. Maybe that's why they feel so uncanny when we find ourselves alone in them.

I find myself drawn to the emotional quality of liminality too — not just physical spaces, but temporal ones. That weird week between Christmas and New Year's when time feels suspended. The summer after graduation before real life begins. Sunday evenings. These moments exist in a strange pocket dimension where normal rules don't quite apply.

What fascinates me is why we find these experiences so compelling, even unsettling. Perhaps it's because liminal spaces strip away context and purpose. A mall without shoppers isn't really a mall anymore — it's just architecture, exposed and strange. We see the scaffolding of our world without its usual meaning.

Or maybe liminal spaces resonate because they mirror something true about existence itself. We're all in transit, really, passing through stages and phases, never quite arriving. These empty hallways and quiet hours are just honest about what we usually prefer to ignore: the in-between is where we spend most of our lives.
